articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Early colored baseball: Excelsiors, Bachelors and Pythians
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Aug. 24, 1912) History of Philadelphia's pioneer colored baseball clubs after the Civil War: the Excelsiors (1866), the Bachelors, and the Pythians — promoted by James H. Francis, James F. Needham and Francis Wood, sponsored by Octavius V. Catto, Jacob C. White Jr., David Knight and J. Whipper Purnell. Octavius V. Catto was the Pythians' captain and manager; games were played near 11th and Wharton and against visiting clubs including the Mutuals and Alerts of Washington (contestants included Prof. Hugh M. Brown, William H. Harris, Major Charles R. Douglass — son of Frederick Douglass — and George F. T. Cook). Col. James M. Auter and Joseph Cline noted as survivors of the Excelsiors.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The Emancipation celebrations compared
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Nov. 1, 1913) Assessment of the 1913 Emancipation semi-centennial celebrations in Pennsylvania, Illinois, New Jersey, New York and Connecticut, weighing their historical presentation of the race's progress.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Samuel Ringgold Ward and the disenfranchised generation
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (Mar. 2, 1912) Quotes an old account of Pennsylvania's constitution allowing colored suffrage in principle while custom, prejudice or design kept Black men from the polls. Highlights Samuel Ringgold Ward as one of the ablest men of the decades between 1830 and 1850, citing Campbell's 'Negro-Mania' on his powers as a debater.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Little Wesley (A.M.E. Zion) Church at eighty-four
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar (Pencil Pusher)
articles.summary: (May 30, 1914) On the jubilee of ('Little') Wesley A.M.E. Zion Church under Rev. Dr. Wallace — founded ~1830 on the north side of Lombard Street between Fifth and Sixth, in the heart of the old Black quarter; the original building later became a Jewish synagogue.
